title: Deep Learning-Based Brain Tumor Detection and Classification from MRI Images Using CNN
authors: M. Muthalakshmi, Karri Venkata Reddy, S. Vishalakshi, M Surya Venkata Nagendra, Aparna S
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Multi-Agent Systems for Collaborative Intelligence (ICMSCI)
abstract: In a humanoid system, primary functions are governed by the brain, where the unnatural growth and division of brain cells can give rise to brain tumors, potentially progressing into cancerous growths. Computer vision offers numerous advantages for human health due to its independence from flawless human judgment. The most reliable diagnostic methods include X-rays, CT scans, and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). A blend of convolutional neural networks (CNN) and binary thresholding methods has been used to ensure accurate detection of tumors. Searching manually for brain malignancies within Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) images is laborious and error-prone in today's context. Moreover, distinguishing tumor locations can be challenging since brain tumor cells closely resemble normal tissue. This system utilizes three datasets - training, testing, and validation - to ascertain whether an individual has a brain tumor. Anticipated results indicate that the proposed approach will significantly outperform its competitors.

title: Development of a Rapid Measurement Device for Hot-State Winding Resistance in Transformer Temperature-Rise Tests
authors: Junlei Liu, Hanwen Ou, Jinchao Wu, Xinyao Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Energy, Power and Grid (ICEPG)
abstract: The transformer temperature-rise test is an essential method for evaluating the thermal stability and operational reliability of equipment. Rapid measurement of winding hot-state resistance immediately after power-off is critical for obtaining ccurate temperature-rise data. However, traditional proaches require manual disconnection and reconnection of test leads after the power supply is cut, causing a significant drop in winding temperature and thereby compromising the accuracy of the test results. To address this limitation, this paper presents the development of a rapid measurement device for hot-state winding resistance in transformer temperature-rise tests, based on a fast short-circuiting mechanism. The proposed system integrates a low-voltage-side rapid short-circuit device, a non-removable measurement lead and automatic discharge unit, and a dual-channel DC resistance meter, enabling the acquisition of the first valid hot-state resistance value within 30 seconds after power-off. xperimental results demonstrate that the method effectively reduces measurement errors caused by temperature decay and eliminates the influence of residual induced electromotive force on accuracy. This study provides practical engineering value for improving both the precision and the automation level of transformer temperature-rise tests.

title: Research on Frequency Modulation Performance Evaluation and Optimization Strategies for New Energy Power Stations Based on Multi Scenario Feature Analysis
authors: Enhui Xu, Teng Fu, Xiaoli Chen, Haiwei Lin, Chuang Zhou, Xiaoyan Zhao
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Energy, Power and Grid (ICEPG)
abstract: To address variable frequency regulation performance in new energy plants, this paper proposes a multi-scenario evaluation method. A multidimensional index system (meteorology, load, grid, equipment) is constructed to extract key features. Core indicators (response time, steady-state error, etc.) are weighted using a combined AHP-Entropy method, and a multi-scenario weighted model quantifies performance. A case study of a wind farm and PV station shows PV's superiority in steady-state accuracy and stability (scores: 0.81 vs. 0.78). This method effectively characterizes performance across scenarios, providing a reference for operational optimization and grid stability.

title: Intelligent Grievance Categorization and Dynamic Departmental Routing: A Multi-Modal E-Governance Framework
authors: Jeyalakshmi, V Harshath, Yenumula Siva Sandeep
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Innovative Practices in Technology and Management (ICIPTM)
abstract: In the contemporary landscape of urban administration, the paradigm of “Smart Cities” demands a transition from reactive, manual grievance redressal to proactive, intelligent systems. As urban populations swell and civic infrastructure becomes increasingly complex, the traditional mechanisms of handling public complaints—characterized by manual categorization, static routing, and lack of urgency prioritization—have proven insufficient. This research report presents the design, development, and evaluation of an Intelligent Grievance Categorization and Dynamic Departmental Routing System, a comprehensive e-governance solution tailored for the linguistic and infrastructural diversity of the Indian context. The proposed system introduces a novel multi-modal classification framework that integrates Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Computer Vision (CV) to automate the triage of citizen grievances. For textual analysis, the system employs MURIL (Multilingual Universal Representations for Information Retrieval), a state-of-the-art transformer model pre-trained on 17 Indian languages, enabling the accurate processing of code-mixed (Hinglish/Tanglish) and vernacular complaints. For visual evidence, the system utilizes EfficientNet, a highly optimized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), to classify images of civic issues (e.g., potholes, garbage dumps, broken streetlights) and, crucially, to assess visual urgency markers (e.g., fire, accidents). These models are orchestrated via a robust FastAPI backend and a React.js frontend, culminating in a Dynamic Routing Engine that assigns complaints to departmental dashboards (Health, Road, Education, Public Infrastructure) in real-time while prioritizing high-urgency cases. Evaluating the system on a custom dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2, 0 0 0}$</tex> multi-modal samples yielded classification accuracies exceeding 90 outperforming traditional baselines. This report further contextualizes the study within a comprehensive survey of 15 recent research papers (2022-2025), highlighting the system's contribution to reducing administrative latency and enhancing citizen satisfaction in digital governance.

title: Relative Humidity Measurement in 14 cm Thick Concrete Blocks
authors: Juan Rafael Filgueira Guerra, Humberto Dionísio de Andrade, Antonio Luiz P. S. Campos
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd Latin American Conference on Antennas and Propagation (LACAP)
abstract: This work presents a microwave system for measuring relative humidity in 14 cm-thick hollow concrete blocks, aiming to establish a technique for structural integrity monitoring. Five block samples were selected for analysis, with a frequency-selective surface (FSS) employed as the sensing element. Two antenna arrays were designed to capture the frequency responses of the blocks combined with the FSS under different humidity levels. The results demonstrate a strong correlation between frequency variation and relative humidity. Numerical processing and statistical analysis enabled the derivation of a second-order function that models the relationship between resonance frequency and relative humidity. A quadratic regression was performed, yielding a function that satisfies the requirements of the proposed study across the full range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0}-\mathbf{1 0 0 \%}$</tex> relative humidity.

title: A Hybrid RAG–ASP Framework for Power Generation Scheduling
authors: Yingying Zhao, Jianqing Li, Junwei Zhang, Jun Nie, Ning Zhao, Shaoxuan Wang, Huanyu Yang, Jianmin Ji
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Energy, Power and Grid (ICEPG)
abstract: Power-generation scheduling jointly optimizes unit commitment and economic dispatch: it selects binary on/off commitments and power set-points that satisfy engineering constraints while minimizing cost. We introduce a neuro-symbolic framework that combines retrieval-augmented generation with Answer Set Programming. The retrieval component turns documentation into a normalized, coefficient table; while the symbolic component encodes engineering constraints as declarative rules and optimizes total cost, producing schedules that satisfy all encoded constraints and are transparent to audit. Across benchmarks, the method satisfies all constraints and attains optimal or near-optimal costs, outperforming text-only generation on feasibility and total cost.

title: Autonomous Agentic AI Frameworks for Supply Chain Resilience and Decision-Making
authors: Divyansh Gupta
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Innovative Practices in Technology and Management (ICIPTM)
abstract: This paper presents an all-encompassing model for the use of Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Supply Chain Management (SCM), with a focus on autonomic decision making, change management, and distributed intelligence at the network level. Contrary to conventional AI techniques, which use static optimization models, Agentic AI uses multiagent systems that are able to flexibly learn and negotiate. In the domain of supply chain, including procurement, production, logistics and inventory management activities, would be able to operate in a dynamic and uncertain environment by learning from reinforcement feedback, continuous data flow and negotiation mechanism among agents. A multi-agent coordination engine provides decentralized and cooperative decision-making, which makes organizations more resilient, agile, and reactive. Logistics optimization, privacy-preserving data sharing and interoperability with partners in the supply chain are also part of the framework. Simulation shows the SC model is costeffective, fast recovering from disruptions and more efficient than the classic AI-based SCM models. The study also demonstrates potential applications in global trade, retail, and manufacturing, including the adaptive systems, ethical and governance issues of implementing agentic technologies. Research findings from simulations show that the system reduces decision latency by 22 % while it achieves 16 % faster recovery times when compared to traditional single-agent systems and rule-based SCM systems.

title: Sensor Fusion and Machine Learning Framework for Seated Movement Detection using a Trunk Orthosis
authors: Sripriya N, Kezia H, Vaishnavi R, Kandula Snehitha, Kolli Lohitha Reddy, Kurapatti Rushmitha
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Multi-Agent Systems for Collaborative Intelligence (ICMSCI)
abstract: Proper measurement of movements of the trunk when seated is vital in monitoring the posture and rehabilitation of individuals who use trunk orthoses. This paper proposes a sensor fusion and machine learning system of real time classification of seated positions between inertial and pressure sensors embedded in a trunk orthosis. Multisensor data can be used to improve stability and improve noise by sensor fusion methods. Training a Random Forest classifier was performed on extracted kinematic and pressure features since this algorithm is resistant to nonlinear and sensor effects. The model was able to identify trunk flexion, extension, lateral bending, and neutral position with high precision, which is better than single sensor and single feature models. The possibility of the proposed fusion of machine learning and experimental results validates the development of a system of intelligent orthoses with the ability to respond to the patient through adaptive feedback and rehabilitation monitoring. The strategy facilitates the emergence of smart assistive tools in the future within a clinical and daily setting.

title: Depth-Aware Pothole and Speed Bump Detection Using Integrated MiDaS and YOLO for Autonomous Driving
authors: Md Pamel Talukder, A.N.M Nurullah Masuder Rahman Noushad, Sahab Uddin, Md Maruf Hasan, Yasin Arafat
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Multi-Agent Systems for Collaborative Intelligence (ICMSCI)
abstract: Road-surface anomalies such as potholes and speed bumps pose serious risks to vehicle stability, passenger comfort, and autonomous navigation safety, particularly under challenging real-world conditions where visual cues are weak or misleading. Conventional 2D vision-based detectors rely heavily on appearance features and lack geometric awareness, while monocular depth estimation methods provide geometric context but struggle with semantic discrimination. To address these limitations, this paper proposes MiLO, a depth-aware twostream fusion framework that integrates MiDaS monocular depth estimation with YOLOv8 object detection for robust road-surface anomaly detection and severity estimation. A novel Depth-Aware Filter (DAF) is introduced to emphasize geometry-salient regions and suppress irrelevant background areas, significantly reducing computational overhead while preserving detection accuracy. Furthermore, an ROI-based depth–distance regression module extracts localized depth statistics and employs polynomial regression to convert relative depth values into approximate real-world measurements, enabling reliable estimation of pothole depth and speed bump height. The framework is evaluated on a diverse dataset of 3,256 images, expanded to 23,580 samples through augmentation, covering varied lighting and weather conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that YOLOv8 achieves the best detection performance (mAP@50 = 0.818, F1 = 0.806), DAF improves processing efficiency by 85.22%, and polynomial regression provides the lowest distance estimation error among tested models. Overall, MiLO offers a scalable, camera-only, and realtime solution for depth-aware road-surface anomaly detection, serving as a cost-effective alternative to LiDAR- and stereobased systems for autonomous driving and intelligent transportation applications.

title: Automating UI Optimization through Multi-Agentic Reasoning

title: Real-Time Prediction of School Buses Using Adaptive Clustering and HMM State Transitions
authors: Rashmi Choudhary
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Innovative Practices in Technology and Management (ICIPTM)
abstract: Accurate traffic state identification is fundamental to intelligent transportation systems, yet traditional clustering approaches often produce unstable results on skewed speed distributions in real-world GPS data. This paper presents a probabilistic framework for school bus networks combining percentile-based K-means clustering with Hidden Markov Model state transitions for adaptive real-time traffic prediction. GPS observations are map-matched to OpenStreetMap segments using Valhalla, enabling clustering based on road characteristics rather than raw coordinates. To address K-means initialization sensitivity on skewed distributions, we introduce percentile-based initialization plus adaptive cluster selection via multiple criteria including silhouette score greater than 0.4 and minimum inter-cluster separation of at least <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2. 0}$</tex> meters per second. We model traffic state evolution as a first-order Markov process, learning transition probabilities between clusters from historical trajectories. An adaptive triggering mechanism selectively activates state propagation when live observations deviate significantly from expectations, propagating congestion effects through downstream segments. Evaluation on million GPS traces from school bus operations demonstrates our integrated approach achieves 90% accuracy within 2-minute tolerance and mean absolute error of 1.87 minutes, representing 8 percentage points improvement over static clustering at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 2 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2}$</tex> points better than commercial routing APIs at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 8 \%}$</tex>. The probabilistic framework enables accurate multi-segment predictions by capturing spatial traffic dependencies that static methods cannot represent.

title: TheraMind AI: A Multilingual and Multidomain Diagnostic Assistant for Psychotherapy
authors: Jajapuram Akshitha Reddy, Yasmeen, Kavitha Goura, Y C A Padmanabha Reddy, K. Mary Sudha Rani, Y.Rama Devi
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Innovative Practices in Technology and Management (ICIPTM)
abstract: TheraMind AI will serve as a modular platform for conversing, capable of sensing emotions and aimed at facilitating psychotherapy-related dialogues within a multilingual and diverse cultural context. The architecture of the system will incorporate emotion detection, retrieval of evidence-based knowledge, and the synthesis of safe responses. Rather than functioning as a fully-fledged diagnostic tool, this work focuses on validating an initial analytical component of the platform, specifically concerning the automated identification of emotions in brief text conversations. An emotion detection module is suggested, utilizing a multilingual DistilBERT model that has been fine-tuned on datasets annotated with emotional content. Despite the issue of class imbalance, the study addresses the semantic connections among various fine-grained emotional labels by aligning the original labels with an aggregated set of broader, high-level emotional categories. The outcome indicates a balanced performance index across the classes, as evidenced by a prominent diagonal in the confusion matrices and elevated macro and weighted F1-scores. This finding confirms the capability of multilingual Transformers to effectively recognize the various emotional states that can be easily identified using affect-sensing dialogue systems, which serves as a crucial basis for a dialogue platform in psychotherapy.

title: Submicron Non-contact Warpage Measurement and Finite Element Analysis of PCBA

title: A Compact Hybrid Log-Periodic Dipole Array Antenna in the Digital TV Band
authors: Carlos Alberto Ramirez Behaine, Giovani Bulla, Abdou-Halique A. A. Bouari, Juner Menezes Vieira
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd Latin American Conference on Antennas and Propagation (LACAP)
abstract: This manuscript proposes a hybrid antenna with filamentary and planar LPDA characteristics. The central axis is a flexible flat base that uses two layers of conductors and a layer of dielectric. The set of dipoles is aerially exhibited, and the thickness is considered for impedance matching. The prototype shows a size of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$29.7 \times 31.2 \times 0.48 ~\text{cm}^{3}$</tex> with a weight of 0.44 pounds. The performance, in terms of the realized gain, varies approximately between 3.8 and 8.0 dBi, which is a promising result compared to state-of-the-art antenna models of similar boom length, in the frequency band of digital TV service of interest, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 7 0 ~ M H z}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 9 0 ~ M H z}$</tex>.

title: 1D Phaseless Inverse Scattering using a Pruned Neural Network
authors: Florindo Bevilacqua, Amedeo Capozzoli, Claudio Curcio, Angelo Liseno
publish: 2025 IEEE Conference on Antenna Measurements and Applications (CAMA)
abstract: We address the problem of reconstructing a one-dimensional object from phaseless scattered fields using a Deep Neural Network (DNN)–based inversion. Since, with NNs, subspaces are replaced by subsets, a different amount of information can be required for a successful reconstruction as compared to standard techniques. We examine how pruning a feed-forward DNN can provide insight on the sufficient information to accomplish the specific task considered in this paper.

title: AI-Based Multimodal Depression Detection Using CNN and Hybrid Deep Learning Architectures
authors: Kunal, Shilpi Harnal, Kirandeep Kaur
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Innovative Practices in Technology and Management (ICIPTM)
abstract: Depression is one of the mental disorders that is widespread around the world. It alters one's emotional state, thoughts, and the ability to interact with others. Current clinical evaluations focus on subjective methods, which makes the diagnosis lack consistency. In order to overcome this problem, the growing field of AI has made it possible to automatically detect signs of depression by combining facial and other multimodal data. This research analyses and synthesises the emotion and depression analysis literature, incorporating various deep learning methods, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and hybrid CNN-LSTM architectures. Research shows that classifiers built on visual data from depressed individuals, including facial movements, eyes, and micro expressions, can accurately determine and classify varying degrees of depression. Moreover, classification accuracy is significantly enhanced with the use of additional data comprising EEG and voice. The AI Classifier aims to support clinically underserved populations and assist in the first-stage diagnosis and ongoing monitoring of depression. This is a cross-section of behavioural science and AI, and it strives to make mental health evaluations more objective.

title: Secure and Scalable Edge—Cloud Architecture for IIOT Energy Monitoring and Anomaly Detection
authors: P. William, Smita Nirkhi, Mohammed Almakki, Prashant V. Thokal, Shanthi Kumaraguru, Bhagyashree Ashok Tingare
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Innovative Practices in Technology and Management (ICIPTM)
abstract: In today's fast-evolving industrial world, where smart factories, energy grids, and automation are all deeply connected, managing energy in real time has become both essential and challenging. The growing number of devices and the data they generate make traditional, centralized systems too slow and vulnerable. This paper introduces a smart and secure Edge-Cloud architecture that solves these challenges by blending fast local data processing (at the edge) with powerful, centralized analysis in the cloud. This hybrid system makes it possible to detect energy issues quickly, keep operations running smoothly, and gain deeper insights through artificial intelligence. The framework has been tested and shows impressive results-detecting anomalies with over 96% accuracy while supporting large—scale deployment and maintaining high system uptime. With this approach, industries can respond faster, stay secure, and make smarter decisions—all in real time.

title: A Relational Learning Approach to Network Intrusion Detection using Graph Neural Networks
authors: M T Vasumathi, V Asha, Sukanya NS, Kavya G M, Jaweriya Mohammadi, Keerthana G R
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Multi-Agent Systems for Collaborative Intelligence (ICMSCI)
abstract: The sophistication and coordination of cyber-attacks increase with the ever-increasing complexity of computer networks. The traditional IDS approaches fail to detect multistage and highly coordinated attacks. This work compares Autoencoder, SVM, Neural Network, and Graph Neural Network-based IDS models using the CTU-13 dataset. Unlike other conventional methods, the GNN model represents the hosts as nodes and network traffic as edges and hence jointly captures both feature information and the underlying structure of the network. Experimental results show that GNN-based IDS outperforms traditional models in accuracy and precision and especially in recall, proving its superiority in identifying various types of network attacks.

title: Automated Plastic Type Classification Using Transfer Learning with Mobilenetv2 for Waste Management Applications
authors: R. Ganesan, Umamaheswari R, Chenchireddygari Sindhu, Komaragiri Leela Siva Brahmachari
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Multi-Agent Systems for Collaborative Intelligence (ICMSCI)
abstract: Recyclability of plastic waste along with the environment's sustainability is highly dependent on effective waste sorting. This paper discusses a deep learning method that brings about the automated classification of the seven types of plastics (HDPE, LDPE, PET, PP, PS, PVC, and Other) by means of transfer learning with the MobileNetV2 model. In the suggested system, the use of pretrained weights from ImageNet and the application of extensive data augmentation are the key points to addressing the limitations imposed by the small size of the training dataset. The proposed methodology has managed to achieve a test accuracy of 75.94% on the benchmark plastic classification dataset, which consists of a total of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1, 8 1 1}$</tex> images split into training, validation, and test sets. Due to the light-weight property of MobileNetV2, the solution is ideal for usage in edge devices and real-time waste sorting systems. Transfer learning is shown by the experimental results to significantly speed up convergence and enhance generalization in comparison to training from scratch. The analysis of the confusion matrix brings to light the strong performance in the major plastic categories but also the difficulties in telling apart the visually similar types of polymers. This study is a stepping stone to the increasing number of research projects that aim to develop AI-based environmental solutions and a source of practical frameworks for industrial waste management applications.

title: Explainable Machine Learning for Predicting Organizational Behaviour: Role of Trust in Management and Colleagues in SMEs
authors: Vivek Vinayakrao Wankhede, Vinod Ramchandra Malkar, Satish Vitthalrao Bidgar
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Innovative Practices in Technology and Management (ICIPTM)
abstract: Organizational Citizenship Behaviour (OCB) is a critical contributor to organizational effectiveness, particularly in small and medium enterprises (SMEs) where discretionary employee efforts significantly influence outcomes. While existing research has largely relied on explanatory models to study antecedents of OCB, limited attention has been given to its predictive assessment using data-driven approaches. Addressing this gap, the present study applies explainable machine learning techniques to predict OCB by examining the role of trust in management and trust in colleagues. Primary data were collected from employees working in SMEs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{N}=\mathbf{3 9 7})$</tex>. The measurement instruments demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>0.98$</tex>) and satisfactory construct validity. Structural Equation Modelling (SEM) results indicated an excellent model fit (RMSEA <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx \mathbf{0. 0 3) }$</tex> and revealed a strong positive association between trust in management and trust in colleagues. Building on this validated structure, explainable machine learning models-including logistic regression, decision tree, and random forest-were employed to predict organizational citizenship behaviour. The results indicate that trust in management and trust in colleagues consistently emerge as significant predictors of OCB across models. Tree-based models demonstrate comparatively better predictive performance while maintaining interpretability through feature-importance analysis. The findings highlight the centrality of trust dynamics in shaping citizenship behaviour and demonstrate the practical value of explainable machine learning as a decision-support approach for organizational behaviour research in SMEs.

title: TFMD: General and Fast Secure Neural Network Inference Framework with Threshold FHE
authors: Yu Fu, Yu Tong, Yijing Ning, Jingqiang Lin, Dengguo Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Secure neural network inference is the privacy-preserving inference method that protects the model parameters and user’s private input. Previous works have constructed two-party, three-party and four-party secure inference schemes. However, these schemes allow only one corrupted party. Also, the interaction protocol between different parties is customized based on the number of participants. If the number of participants increases or decreases, the protocol needs to be redesigned. Another problem is that current protocols for non-linear functions still have large computation overhead. In this work, we present TFMD, a general and fast secure neural network inference framework with semi-honest security. TFMD is built based on threshold fully homomorphic encryption (FHE), and is suitable for the outsourced computation scenario. Concretely, TFMD designs general secure computation protocols for non-linear functions. Our protocols support arbitrary n participants, and allow at most n – 1 corrupted parties. Further, TFMD constructs a novel secure neural network inference framework. TFMD employs FHE with computation-friendly coefficient encoding to quickly calculate linear functions, and employs our proposed protocol to calculate ReLU. Experiments illustrate that TFMD is both efficient and scalable. Even in the three-party setting, the online phase of our inference is 2.1× faster than CrypTFlow (S&P’20).

title: Investigating Secondary School Students’ Use of AI-based Intelligent Technology in Mathematics Learning: A Mixed-Methods Study Using the Technology Acceptance Model
authors: Sicheng Xie, Shuhui Li, Qiaoping Zhang
abstract: The rapid integration of artificial intelligence (AI) has led to the increasing use of AI-based intelligent technology in various disciplines and educational contexts. Yet, little is known about how K–12 students accept and use such technologies in mathematics learning. This study examined secondary students’ adoption of AI-based intelligent technology through an extended Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) that incorporated subjective norms, perceived usefulness, perceived ease of use, behavioral intention, and actual use. A mixed-methods design was employed, combining survey data from 483 Chinese secondary students with followed semi-structured interviews with 12 participants. Confirmatory factor analysis and structural equation modelling were employed to analyze the quantitative data. Results revealed that subjective norms were the strongest predictor of behavioral intention, followed by perceived usefulness, while perceived ease of use had a weaker yet significant effect. Behavioral intention significantly influenced actual use, and qualitative findings further validated the extended TAM. This study provides new insights into students’ acceptance of AI-based intelligent technology in mathematics education, highlighting the pivotal role of subjective norms and perceived usefulness in shaping students’ adoption. Implications are discussed in the end, aiming to advance the effective integration of AI-based intelligent technology in K–12 schools.

title: Edge-Based Robot-Assisted Hand Gesture Recognition for Autism Intervention Using Hybrid Keyframe Selection and Contrastive Learning
authors: Sougatamoy Biswas, Soumyajeet Mahapatra, Anup Nandy, Asim Kumar Naskar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) often face challenges in expressing and interpreting social gestures essential for social skill development. Traditional approaches depend on intrusive wearable sensors and manual supervision, with minimal adaptation to real-time, resource-constrained environments. To address these limitations, we present a real-time vision-based human–robot interaction (HRI) framework that autonomously supports gesture learning and correction without therapist intervention. The system employs MediaPipe-based skeletal landmark extraction with a novel hybrid keyframe selection strategy. This strategy integrates entropy-based filtering to retain semantically rich frames and a clustering-based mechanism to identify representative frames from similar gesture groups. Spatial features are modeled using one-dimensional convolutions across hand joints to capture intra-frame structural dependencies. The framework executes gesture classification and correctness evaluation simultaneously, with contrastive learning incorporated to enhance the separability of learned embeddings. An in-house assistive robot, named CARAT (Caregiver Autism Robot with AI-based Technology), has been developed to realize this framework. CARAT is equipped with a gesture-performing arm, deployed on a Jetson Orin Nano edge device, and provides real-time verbal feedback alongside physical gesture demonstrations. The dataset for evaluation was collected using CARAT, involving participants from both healthy children and children with ASD. The framework demonstrates strong recognition performance, achieving 98.85% accuracy with healthy participants, 96.42% with autistic participants, and 97.41% on the combined dataset of both groups. These results validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach for hand gesture recognition in edge-deployed assistive robots for autism intervention.

title: Exploiting Angular Diversity For Passive Positioning VLC Systems
authors: Alejandro López Barrios, Borja Genovés Guzmán, Máximo Morales-Céspedes
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Retroreflective visible light communications (R-VLC) have been recently proposed for providing ultra-low power positioning and communications in the framework of the Internet of Things. Specifically, devices equipped with a corner-cube retroreflector send optical signal back to the source of light through a parallel path, where the photodetector (PD) is co-located. To obtain positioning through trilateration methods, previous works consider a large separation among PDs so that they provide at least three linearly independent channel responses. In this work, we propose a R-VLC system based on the deployment of PDs following an angular diversity arrangement onto the source plane. This approach achieves two goals; i) it enables trilateration-based positioning for small sources of light by implementing a single angle diversity receiver (ADR) and following a centralized structure and ii) it improves the footprint and precision of positioning accuracy, i.e., the area in which positioning is accurate, compared to traditional approaches. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme increases the positioning area 60%, ensuring a positioning accuracy of 1 cm. Moreover, it is also shown that positioning performance can be improved increasing the number of PDs and carefully selecting them to avoid correlated channel responses.

title: Robust Finite Control Set Using Sliding Mode Control for Induction Motor for Agricultural Electrical Traction
authors: Rafael Moreno, Rafael Figueiredo, Igor Oliani, Lucas Augusto, Yuri Cota, José A. Torrico, Alfeu J. Sguarezi Filho
abstract: Electric vehicles for off-road agricultural applications utilize induction motors due to their low cost, simplicity, and efficiency of widely developed control methods, but with challenges in the presence of external factors such as roads with irregularities or internal factors like variations in motor parameters. Given the necessity for robust electric vehicles capable of operating reliably in agricultural environments this paper proposes a robust finite control set (FCS) with sliding mode control (SMC) with PI for predictive current control of induction motor and its application in agricultural electrical traction. The cost function sums the predicted voltages in the stator reference frame from the motor with the robust control voltages and compares them with the voltages of the converter to maintain the proposed system’s current reference. The proposed robust FCS-SMC will be compared to the classical finite control set and robust finite control set using the deadbeat approach. Considering that there are several more complex robust controls, our work focuses on the advantages that sliding mode offers, such as ease of implementation in addition to being a novelty in the literature of the use of sliding mode control with finite control set theory in stationary frame for current control. Experimental tests were developed to evaluate the proposed control, the results show the alpha current component within an adequate operating range, the parametric mismatch test with total harmonic distorsion under 4.1%, the mechanical speed test within acceptable values with low error and computational burden of 61% validates the proposed system.

title: Multi-Agent Coverage Using Multiplicatively Weighted Energy Voronoi Partitions
authors: Bilal Yousuf, Lucian Buşoniu, Irinel-Constantin Morǎrescu
abstract: We propose a coverage control method for energy-constrained multi-agent systems with single-integrator agent motion, in which agent energy discharges and recharges at constant rates. Differently existing methods, a Multiplicatively Weighted Energy Voronoi (MWEV) partition ensures that each agent’s coverage region varies with energy in such a way that it vanishes when energy drops to a reserve level sufficient to reach a charging station. We show that the optima of an energy-aware coverage objective are the dynamically evolving MWEV centroids. A generalized Lloyd algorithm provably drives agents with remaining battery to these centroids, and reserve-level agents to their charging stations, under a switched two-timescale model with fast motion and slow energy dynamics. Agents repeatedly pause coverage as they get depleted, and then resume coverage upon recharging. This happens arbitrarily many times, leading to an infinite-horizon coverage setting. The method works well in simulations, where we also apply an alternative technique with a different problem formulation. We contrast coverage performance and average agent downtime between the two methods; e.g., downtime is 17.70% of the experiment duration for our MWEV technique, compared to 28.5% for the alternative. In addition, the robustness of the proposed approach is investigated under nonlinear battery discharge dynamics, position errors, and delayed energy information.

title: Temporal Conditional Score Network for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection
authors: Hao Zhou, Wentao Xu, Xuan Zhang, Anis Yazidi, Ke Yu
abstract: Time series anomaly detection is a critical technique for secure monitoring in cyber-physical systems (CPSs). Most existing methods learn a normal data profile and identify anomalies as deviations from this profile. Recently, score-based models, which estimate the gradient of the log data probability density, have shown promise in improving data distribution estimation due to their strong generative capabilities. However, their application to time series anomaly detection in CPSs remains underexplored, with key challenges in network architecture design and the accuracy-efficiency trade-off yet to be addressed. In this paper, we propose the Temporal Conditional Score Network (TCSN), a novel framework that jointly estimates conditional scores and detects anomalies based on reconstruction error. Our approach enhances a U-Net backbone with temporal attention skip connections, preserving detailed spatio-temporal features at both variable and time-step levels. Additionally, signature matrix embedding is introduced to improve sensitivity to anomalous patterns. We further establish connections between score-based and reconstruction-based anomaly measurements, leading to a more efficient and accurate detection strategy. To ensure practical applicability, we design a full-cycle deployment pipeline that integrates data sanitization, denoising score matching–based training, hyperparameter tuning, and proactive model updates, enhancing scalability and long-term stability. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that TCSN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to 0.2155 improvement in composition F1 score while maintaining strong robustness under varying noise levels.

title: Dependence of Joint Resistance on Conductor Arrangement for Mechanical Edge Joint in Remountable HTS Fusion Magnets
authors: Motohiko Himura, Satoshi Ito, Nagato Yanagi, Hidetoshi Hashizume
abstract: Remountable (being able to mount and demount repeatedly) high-temperature superconducting (HTS) fusion mag net is a promising concept for the next generation fusion reactors which allows segmented coil fabrication and on-site assembly. However, a joint resistance below 1 nΩ under applied magnetic field needs to be achieved to realize this concept. This study investigated the joint resistance for mechanical edge joint of HTS Stacked Tapes Assembled in Rigid Structure (STARS) conductors. Three-dimensional (3-D) numerical analysis based on the charge conservation law, which accounts for magnetore sistance of conductive materials, was performed to evaluate joint resistance of the single-, double-, and triple-stack arrangements with uniform or symmetrical tape stack structures. The results showed that the symmetrical-double-stack arrangement excels in both its low joint resistance and its performance under a magnetic field. Application to the fusion magnet conductor using many Rare-Earth Barium Copper Oxide (REBCO) tapes was also analyzed through the joint resistance and the maximum flat-wise bending strain applied. The joint resistance of the symmetrical double-stack 72 layers (144 tapes) arrangement yielded 4.3 nΩ with joint length of 100 mm, the load factor I′ of 60%. It satisfied the requirement that joint resistance of 0.794 nΩ at I′ of 30% under the magnetic field of 15 T was obtained with joint length of 1 m.

title: Approximating Sets of Stabilizing Periodic Switching Signals
authors: Ivan O. Shevchenko, Xinzhi Liu
abstract: We propose a method of approximating the set of all T-periodic globally stabilizing switching signals corresponding to a fixed switched linear system. This is accomplished by approximating system solutions using a truncated Chen-Fliess series expansion and imposing successively less restrictive conditions on the spectral radius of the approximate time-T state-transition matrix. For periodic switching signals with at most d switches in one period, we provide an explicit algorithm for constructing these approximating sets and include an analysis of the computational considerations, along with a numerical example. The novelty of our approach is in formulating, in the limit, necessary and sufficient conditions for stability of switched linear systems based solely on the set of admissible switching signals. Our analysis is valid regardless of the eigenvalues of the system matrices.

title: A Novel Wideband HTS BPF With Enhanced Frequency Selectivity and Stopband Rejection
authors: Linping Feng, Jinhai Tang, Xinhua Yu, Lei Zhu, Yinggang Li
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter introduces a novel high-temperature superconducting (HTS) high-order wideband bandpass filter (BPF) with high-passband selectivity and deep stopband rejection. The proposed filter design employs the terminated coupled-line structures (TCLS) to achieve the desired performance characteristics. Initially, we elaborate on the working principle of the utilized TCLS, consisting of a coupled line with four terminals. In contrast to the conventional methods, our approach involves loading a cascade circuit onto two terminals of the coupled line to create two pairs of transmission zeros. In addition, one terminal of the coupled lines is short-circuited to further enhance stopband rejection and bandwidth steepness. Subsequently, we conduct a theoretical analysis of the adopted terminated coupled line structures to validate the feasibility of the approach. The proposed HTS filter is designed to operate at 3.6GHz, and has a fractional bandwidth of 39%, a lower stopband rejection of 61 dB, an upper stopband rejection of 20 dB, and compact dimensions (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.327\lambda _{\text {g}} \times 0.147\lambda _{\text {g}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) on a MgO substrate. Good agreement is observed between the simulated results and the measured ones, which demonstrates good achixevement of the desired performance of the proposed filter.

title: Equivariant Bayesian Hyperspectral Imaging via Mosaiced and PAN Image Fusion
authors: Renwei Dian, Nan Wang, Anjing Guo, Shutao Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: A high-resolution (HR) hyperspectral imaging at video rates can be achieved by fusing a multi-band low-resolution (LR) mosaiced image and a single-band HR panchromatic (PAN) image in a single shot within a short time. However, the current fusion methods always suffer from a spatial or spectral distortion. To alleviate this, we propose an equivariant Bayesian variational inference framework. Specifically, we decompose an HR hyperspectral image (HSI) into the principal component and sparsity residual, which are modeled as latent variables with Gaussian priors. Each component is estimated via a shared deep neural network (DNN) under a variational inference framework, leveraging the shared spatial structures to enhance parameter efficiency and reconstruction accuracy. Additionally, to tackle the challenge of unavailable ground truth in real-world scenarios, we integrate the equivariant imaging (EI) prior with the Bayesian framework. By enforcing the consistency between the transformed fusion result and the re-inference output, this strategy enables the network to learn beyond the range space. Furthermore, we propose to utilize the learnable degradation functions derived from the physical imaging model to enable the proposed framework, which ensures an enhanced performance by posing plausible constraints on parameters of the degradation functions. Specifically, we explicitly model the point spread function (PSF) and spectral response function (SRF) with learnable parameters and impose non-negativity and sum-to-one constraints. Extensive experiments conducted on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework, paving the way for HSI computational imaging.

title: Re-evaluating Virtual Reality Manipulation Techniques for Precise Alignment of Complex 3D Objects_supp2-3679131.mp4
authors: Cherelle Connor
abstract: Prior research has developed a number of manipulation techniques that can achieve precise object placement in virtual reality, but studies of these techniques typically use simple objects. We conducted a study comparing two existing techniques, (AMP-IT and WISDOM), during alignment of objects with complex geometry to evaluate the potential influence of geometric complexity on performance, usability, workload and preference. Our findings indicate that participants had faster completion times and higher trial completion rates with AMP-IT on high-precision alignment tasks, contrary to earlier findings that used simple objects. Yet WISDOM is still preferred and considered more usable, despite increased workload and poorer performance, exposing participants' willingness to trade objective performance for comfort during use.

title: Taking Language Embedded 3D Gaussian Splatting into the Wild_supp2-3680695.mp4
authors: Yue Qi
abstract: Recent advances in leveraging large-scale Internet photo collections for 3D reconstruction have enabled immersive virtual exploration of landmarks and historic sites worldwide. However, existing methods primarily focus on visual appearance reconstruction, often overlooking the interactive semantic understanding of these 3D scenes (e.g., identifying specific building parts or scene details), which remains largely confined to browsing static text-image pairs. Therefore, can we draw inspiration from 3D in-the-wild reconstruction techniques and use unconstrained photo collections to create an immersive approach for comprehensive 3D scene understanding beyond mere visual appearance? To this end, we extend language embedded 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) and propose a novel framework for open-vocabulary scene understanding from unconstrained photo collections. Specifically, we first render multiple appearance images from the same viewpoint as the unconstrained image with the reconstructed radiance field, then extract multi-appearance CLIP features and two types of language feature uncertainty maps-transient and appearance uncertainty-derived from the multi-appearance features to guide the subsequent optimization process. Next, we propose a transient uncertainty-aware autoencoder, a multi-appearance language field 3DGS representation, and a post-ensemble strategy to effectively compress, learn, and fuse language features from multiple appearances. Finally, to quantitatively evaluate our method, we introduce PT-OVS, a new benchmark dataset for assessing open-vocabulary segmentation performance on unconstrained photo collections. Experimental results show that our method outperforms existing methods, delivering accurate open-vocabulary segmentation and enabling applications such as interactive roaming with open-vocabulary queries, architectural style pattern recognition, and 3D scene editing.

title: MB-STFormer: A Multi-Band Spectral-Temporal Transformer with Efficient Attention for Enhanced EEG-Based Fatigue Detection
authors: Ke Liu, Lilong Sun, Wenlong Wang, Zhenghui Gu, Zhuliang Yu, Wei Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Accurate detection of driver fatigue is critical for preventing traffic accidents. Although electroencephalogram (EEG) signals provide a robust physiological indicator of fatigue, effectively capturing their intricate spatiotemporal-spectral dynamics poses significant challenges. In this paper, we propose MB-STFormer, a novel deep neural network designed for EEG-based fatigue detection, which systematically integrates neurophysiological priors into deep feature learning. The proposed MB-STFormer employs a multi-branch frequency-aware module to extract spatiotemporal features from EEG signals, with each branch dedicated to a distinct frequency sub-band. By leveraging adaptive temporal convolution kernel sizes tailored to each sub-band, the model adeptly captures the inherent rhythmic patterns and temporal dynamics unique to different frequency components. Additionally, we introduce an Efficient Additive Attention mechanism to aggregate global contextual information, thereby addressing the over-smoothing of subtle yet critical features often encountered with conventional transformer self-attention mechanisms. Extensive experiments conducted on three publicly available datasets demonstrate that MB-STFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining superior interpretability and generalizability. The proposed framework offers a promising solution for real-world fatigue monitoring systems.

title: Characterizations of Primitive and Projective Self-Orthogonal BCH Codes and Their Parameters
authors: Shuying Dong, Chengju Li, Haifeng Qian
abstract: Self-orthogonal codes are an important type of linear codes since they are very closely related to designs, lattices, and quantum codes. Bose-Chaudhuri-Hocquenghem codes (BCH codes) have various practical applications in communication and storage due to their efficient encoding and decoding algorithms. In this paper, we will focus on the primitive and projective self-orthogonal BCH codes in both Euclidean and Hermitian cases. Our main objective is to characterize primitive and projective Euclidean and Hermitian self-orthogonal BCH codes and investigate their parameters. For the Euclidean case, the primitive and projective self-orthogonal BCH codes are characterized completely by using their designed distances. For the Hermitian case, a sufficient and necessary condition for all primitive BCH codes being self-orthogonal are presented, while the characterizations of projective Hermitian self-orthogonal BCH codes are obtained in some cases. Moreover, the dimensions of some Euclidean and Hermitian self-orthogonal BCH codes are determined explicitly and lower bounds on their minimum distances are given.

title: Design of a Passive Load-Bearing Exoskeleton With Reduced Joint Misalignment and Leg Interaction Forces_supp5-3675446.mp4
authors: xinyu wu
abstract: Load-bearing exoskeletons offer assistance in military, firefighting, and logistics fields. In such scenarios, weights are carried across uneven terrains. Therefore, an exoskeleton that efficiently transfers loads to the ground, adapts to versatile gaits, and minimally impacts leg movement is desired. This article presents a passive load-bearing exoskeleton to achieve these objectives. The passive exoskeleton operates without external power, transfers the load to the ground and redirects interaction forces from the thigh to the waist, and reduces potential impact on leg motion. A hip force-restoring mechanism and an optimized knee four-bar linkage were developed to minimize misalignment, enhancing human–machine kinematic compatibility. Experiments on five participants showed that the exoskeleton can reduce misalignment by 42.55% during load-bearing walking and 79.68% when squatting. The thigh interaction force was decreased by 55.65%, and load-transfer efficiency reached 60.10%. Compared to commercial load-bearing exoskeletons, there was a significant reduction in metabolic cost both indoors and outdoors, showing the exoskeleton's effectiveness in various terrain conditions.

title: Joint Detection on Reconfigurable Photonic Processors with Multi-Input Phase Stabilization
authors: Igor A. Litvin, Gökhan Elmas, Janis Nötzel
abstract: We report a joint-detection (JD) experiment between two reconfigurable photonic processors in which Hadamard codewords are generated on a transmitter processor and a Hadamard transform is performed on a receiver (decoder) processor. Crucially, the decoder executes multi-input phase stabilization concurrently with the optical Hadamard combining, ensuring the phase alignment required for coherent joint detection. We demonstrate JD using <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2{\times }2$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4{\times }4$</tex-math></inline-formula> Hadamard transformations and verify stable temporal outputs under active on-chip feedback control (FBC). The approach leverages our multi-input FBC scheme to suppress low-frequency inter-input phase fluctuations and to realize coherent combining consistent with Green-Machine style architectures. These results indicate a practical path to scalable, codeword-level optical joint detection on programmable photonic hardware and are compatible with larger-mode processors and higher-order code families.

title: Predictive Gait Generation Based on Passive Morphology: Kinematic and Kinetic Optimization
authors: Mahan Jaberi, Emily Rogers-Bradley
abstract: Objective: This study investigates whether the inherent morphology of a passive dynamic walking system can accurately reproduce human gait kinematics and kinetics and assesses its potential for predictive gait generation. Methods: A multistage optimization algorithm was implemented to tune monoarticular and biarticular elastic and viscoelastic components representing the quasi-stiffness of lower-limb muscles. The resulting model was evaluated against an open source gait dataset from six healthy adults with comparable body mass and walking speeds. Resemblance was quantified using the coefficient of determination (\boldmath $R^{2}$) for joint kinematics and kinetics at three gait speeds of approximately 0.83 m/s, 0.96 m/s, and 1.12 m/s corresponding to the lower, intermediate, and upper stable slope angle limits. Results: Purely passive gaits replicated human hip and knee joint angles with average \boldmath $R^{2}$ of 0.98 and 0.99 respectively. When optimized for joint moments, the model maintained strong kinematic agreement and produced ankle and hip joint moments with average $R^{2}$ of 0.94 and 0.60 across corresponding speeds. Ankle joint angle and knee joint moment, however, had room for improvement with average \boldmath $R^{2}$ of 0.45 and 0.01 respectively. Conclusion: A passive dynamic walking model with a novel optimization algorithm and elastic elements replicating lower-limb muscles, can reproduce human gait kinematics and kinetics with high accuracy, demonstrating that morphology alone can account for essential features of locomotor dynamics. Significance: This work highlights the role of morphology in human locomotion and introduces a simple, accessible framework for predictive gait generation to evaluate the expected biomechanical impact of assistive and rehabilitative devices.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Data-Driven Approaches in University Science Education: A Review of Applications and Outcomes
authors: Kadek Dwi Hendratma Gunawan, Jovita Ridhani, Annisa Nur Khasanah, Bayu Antrakusuma, Putu Harry Gunawan, Muhammad Nur Hudha
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Geoinformatics (ICDSG)
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and data-driven approaches into higher education has accelerated in recent years, particularly within science education, where traditional models often struggle with scalability, diverse learning needs, and assessment quality. This review examines applications of artificial intelligence (AI)—encompassing both classical machine-learning systems and emerging generative AI models—and data-driven methods in university-level science education. It uniquely contributes by mapping recent empirical evidence (2016–2025) through a governance–readiness– implementation lens that links technical deployment to pedagogical and ethical outcomes. A narrative review of peer-reviewed literature published between 2016 and 2025 was conducted, drawing on studies that explore the pedagogical, technological, and institutional dimensions of AI adoption. The findings indicate that AI is most commonly employed in learning analytics, predictive modeling, adaptive learning systems, and AI-enabled assessment and feedback, with additional applications in immersive laboratory environments and institutional decision-making. Across studies, these approaches often enhance personalization, support at-risk students, streamline faculty workload, and enable curriculum reform. Reported outcomes include improved academic performance, higher engagement, and greater student autonomy; however, these benefits are tempered by challenges relating to equity, data ethics, algorithmic bias, and sustainability. The review highlights that AI and data-driven education represent transformative yet ethically sensitive innovations whose effectiveness depends on governance frameworks, educator readiness, and responsible implementation. Universities are thus called to adopt AI not merely as a technical enhancement but as a pedagogical partner, ensuring its integration aligns with equity, integrity, and evidence-based learning. By synthesizing cross-disciplinary findings, this study extends prior reviews by delineating evidence strength and ethical governance mechanisms specific to STEM domains.

title: Systematic Literature Review - Prediction of Construction Project Delays Using the Random Forest Algorithm
authors: Ni Ketut Asriwinarti
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Geoinformatics (ICDSG)
abstract: Construction project delays remain one of the most critical challenges in the global construction industry, often resulting in cost overruns, contractual conflicts, and reduced stakeholder satisfaction. Traditional approaches to delay analysis have shown limitations in handling complex and nonlinear project data, prompting increasing interest in the application of machine learning (ML) techniques for predictive analytics. This study aims to systematically review the application of the Random Forest (RF) algorithm in predicting construction project delays to provide a comprehensive understanding of its performance and research trends. Using the PRISMA methodology, 28 peer-reviewed articles published between 2023 and 2025 were identified and analyzed based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. The review addresses three main research questions: identifying key delay factors, understanding RF-based modeling approaches, and evaluating comparative algorithm performance. The findings reveal that the most common factors contributing to construction delays include activity duration, cost performance, contractor productivity, weather variability, and administrative efficiency. RF has been successfully utilized to capture nonlinear relationships and manage high-dimensional project data, achieving high prediction accuracy—often above 85%—in most studies. Compared to other ML models such as Decision Trees, Support Vector Machines, and Neural Networks, RF consistently demonstrates superior performance and robustness. Furthermore, hybrid RF models incorporating optimization techniques like Genetic Algorithms or feature-selection mechanisms show enhanced predictive accuracy and reduced error rates. This review contributes to advancing knowledge on data-driven delay prediction and reinforces the role of RF as a reliable and adaptable tool for proactive project risk management.

title: Small Spot Size and High Transmission Cold Cathode Electron Gun Based on Sharp Point Tungsten Emitter With Suppressor Electrode
authors: Wenrui Liang, Shuai Tang, Junhao Zhong, Caijian Zhang, Yan Shen, Yu Zhang, Shaozhi Deng
abstract: Cold cathode electron guns have demonstrated significant advantages of high resolution and high brightness in vacuum electronic devices. However, the tradeoff between electron transmittance and focal spot size (FSS) is a big challenge. This article proposes a scheme of using a sharp cathode and a suppressor electrode to achieve a small FSS and high transmittance in a cold cathode electron gun simultaneously. Through initial finite-element simulations of an electron beam, the electron gun with a sharp cathode shows much localized electric field and induces an original convergent electron beam. The electron gun with suppressor electrode reduces the electric field gradient on the cathode surface and generates a much focused beam combined with the gate electrode. The influence of the suppressor electrode and the cathode curvature radius on the electron transmittance and electron FSS is measured. The results show that after introducing the suppressor electrode structure and using a cathode with a smaller radius of curvature, the electron beam FSS has decreased by 53.8%, and the electron transmittance has increased by 12.2%. At present, the electron gun with a sharp tungsten field emitter of tip radius of 10 nm and suppressor electrode shows an electron beam FSS of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$88~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m and an electron transmittance of 42.7% under an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. The results provide a feasible solution for achieving high electron transmission and small FSS of cold cathode guns.

title: PERFECG-Net: A Lightweight Periodicity-focused Fetal ECG R-Peak Detection Network
authors: Durgesh Ameta, Sanjay Somasundaram, S Srivarshini, Amit Shukla
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Reliable detection of fetal R-peaks from abdominal electrocardiography (AECG) is essential for accurate and continuous fetal heart rate (FHR) monitoring, particularly in point-of-care and wearable healthcare applications. Conventional signal processing approaches often operate offline and degrade under low signal-to-noise conditions, while existing deep learning-based solutions typically rely on large, computationally intensive architectures, limiting their suitability for real-time deployment. To address these challenges, we propose PERFECG-Net (PERiodicity-Focused Fetal ECG Network), a lightweight end-to-end framework for fetal R-peak detection that explicitly embeds physiological cardiac periodicity into its architecture. The core component, a Physiologically-Informed Convolutional Bank (PICB), consists of multiple convolutional kernels aligned with physiologically plausible fetal heart rate ranges, enabling efficient extraction of rhythmic fetal ECG patterns. Despite its compact design with fewer than 150K parameters, PERFECG-Net achieves competitive detection performance across benchmark fetal ECG datasets while maintaining low computational complexity and real-time inference capability. To further evaluate edge deployment feasibility, the proposed model was deployed on an ESP32 microcontroller, demonstrating stable detection performance with real time inference. These results demonstrate that incorporating physiological priors into an ultra-lightweight architecture enables accurate, robust, and deployable fetal heart monitoring suitable for continuous clinical and home-based use. The source codes and trained models are available at: https://github.com/SSanjay0614/PERFECG-Net.

title: A Unified Approach for the Design of Accurate Torque Controllers for Electrically Excited Synchronous Motors
authors: Alessandro Ionta, Sandro Rubino, Federica Graffeo, Fausto Stella, Radu Bojoi
abstract: Permanent magnet synchronous machines (PMSMs) with rare-earth magnets are widely recognized as the preferred choice for electric powertrains due to their high torque density and efficiency. However, in an effort to reduce reliance on rare-earth materials, electrically excited synchronous machines (EESMs) have emerged as a promising alternative for auto motive applications. EESMs offer enhanced control flexibility over PMSMs, as their magnetic flux can be actively regulated through the rotor field current. The existing literature provides several torque control solutions for EESM, such as direct current control of the machine's currents and hybrid solutions that directly control the stator flux amplitude and torque. These torque control approaches (TCA) seem to lead to very different control structures, requiring a case-by-case design. However, this article proposes a unified approach specifically designed for traction EESMs that enables selecting the most common TCA reported in the literature while preserving accurate torque regulation and minimizing total copper loss. The effectiveness of the proposed solution is experimentally validated using a commercially available 100 kW EESM, which is employed in the Renault Zoe EV R135.

title: Abnormal Degradations in a-InGaZnO TFTs Caused by Metal Reaction-Induced Doping under Self-Heating Stress
authors: Bo-Shen Huang, Ting-Chang Chang, Yu-An Chen, Ya-Ting Chien, Cheng-Ting Tsai, Tsung-Ming Tsai, Yang-Shun Fan, Chen-Shuo Huang, Chih-Hung Tsai
abstract: In this work, the abnormal degradation behaviors in amorphous InGaZnO (a-IGZO) thin-film transistors (TFTs) under self-heating stress (SHS) are discussed. Under SHS, it is observed that the threshold voltage (Vth) first shifts to the left and then to the right, accompanied by the appearance of a hump in the capacitance–voltage (C–V) measurement. During the subsequent recovery time, Vth continues to shift to the right, while the hump in the C–V measurement gradually disappears. Moreover, the results indicate that these degradation behaviors are related to both temperature and gate voltage. Among the observed degradations, the right shift of Vth is attributed to electron trapping into the gate insulator (GI), whereas the remaining phenomena originate from H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O molecules and their non-uniform distribution at the back interface of the channel, which become space charges under SHS. The presence of H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O molecules is due to insufficient protection by the SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> passivation layer, whereas their non-uniform distribution can be traced back to the metal reaction-induced doping process during device fabrication. The proposed model is further confirmed by extracting the length of the pre-accumulation region from normalized C–V curves under SHS with varying channel lengths.

authors: Tommaso Calò, Luigi De Russis

title: Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation for Fast-Food Quality Control: A Dual-Student Approach with SegFormer
authors: Matheus Ramos De Carvalho, André Sacilotti, Ricardo José Ferrari
abstract: This paper presents a cost-effective Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (WSSS) framework for quality control in fast-food sandwich assembly. The proposed approach reduces pixel-level annotation costs by over 85% by leveraging a dual-student architecture with a SegFormer-B0 backbone. In contrast to prior WSSS studies that primarily target academic benchmarks, this work emphasizes industrial deployability, focusing on robustness, computational efficiency, and scalability under real-world constraints. The framework integrates curriculum learning, cross-supervision between siamese networks, and improved pseudo-label generation via test-time augmentation, alongside balanced sampling strategies to address the severe class imbalance of the Visio Sandwich Segmentation dataset, which consists of low-resolution images acquired from closed-circuit television cameras. Experimental results indicate that the proposed method achieves segmentation performance that is consistently competitive with state-of-the-art dual-student WSSS approaches, exhibiting lower variance across evaluation folds while maintaining substantially reduced annotation cost. In addition, the framework supports high-throughput inference, processing more than 1,100 images per second with minimal video random-access memory usage. Overall, the results demonstrate that lightweight transformer-based WSSS can provide a favorable trade-off between accuracy, robustness, annotation efficiency, and computational performance, making it suitable for large-scale industrial food inspection systems.
