title: Integrating Physiological and Metabolic Indicators for Heart Disease Risk Prediction Using Machine Learning Approaches
authors: I Gede Septa Adi Gunajaya, Ni Gede Dwita Putri Shanti, I Wayan Galang Utama Giri, I Gede Abi Bhakti Paramartha, Gde Palguna Reganata
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Geoinformatics (ICDSG)
abstract: Heart disease remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, emphasizing the need for innovative and non-invasive screening approaches for early detection. Fatigue, a multidimensional physiological and psychological state, has been linked to autonomic nervous system imbalance and cardiovascular risk. Pupil diameter reflects sympathetic and parasympathetic activity, making pupil changes a promising biomarker for assessing fatigue and related cardiovascular conditions. This study aimed to develop a machine learning–based screening model integrating pupil response, fatigue indicators, and metabolic parameters to predict early risk of heart disease. Data was obtained from experimental pupillary measurements and public clinical datasets, including blood pressure, cholesterol, and glucose levels. Logistic Regression, Artificial Neural Network (ANN), and XGBoost algorithms were applied to construct screening models, with ensemble soft voting used for performance optimization. The integrated model achieved good predictive accuracy (AUC 0.79) in distinguishing high-and low-risk categories, demonstrating that pupil-based physiological features can complement traditional risk markers. Pupil changes exhibit dilation in individuals with moderate fatigue and shift toward pupil contriction in those with high fatigue which is related with cardiovascular risk. The findings suggest that the screening model provides a non-invasive, data-driven screening approach for early identification of individuals at potential cardiovascular risk. This approach may enhance preventive strategies and facilitate personalized health monitoring using accessible physiological indicators.

title:
First-Principles Study of N
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    or H
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    /N
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Plasma Pretreatment: Effects on the Interface Properties in Si
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    N
                    <sub>4</sub>
                    /AlN/GaN MIS-HEMTs
authors: Jiaofen Yang, Ming Tao, Jing Xiao, Kai Tang, Yilan Chen, Wenjing Zhang, Bin Zhang, Bowen Huang, Jie Liu, Hongyue Wang, Jinyan Wang, Maojun Wang
abstract: This work investigates the effects of N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> and H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> plasma pretreatment (PP) on the gate interface formation and interface defects in Si<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub>/AlN/GaN metal–insulator–semiconductor high-electron-mobility transistors (MISHEMTs) through first-principles calculations. Based on STEM, EDX, and EELS, the interface structures with various O/N ratios are constructed to simulate no PP, N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP, and H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP on the etched GaN surface. The GaN surface with no PP or N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP exhibits a high O/N ratio exceeding 66.7%, facilitating the growth of N-polar AlN. This AlN layer is almost entirely oxidized, ultimately forming an Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/GaN interface. In contrast, H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP achieves the GaN surface with a low O/N ratio below 66.7%, promoting the growth of Al-polar AlN, which exhibits significantly lower oxidation. Consequently, the Al-polar-AlN/GaN and Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/GaN interfaces are ultimately formed. For the Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/GaN interfaces formed in different PP processes, compared to no PP, both N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP and H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP can reduce Ga, O, and Al dangling bonds (DBs) but increase N DBs, thereby suppressing interface defects to some extent. However, an almost defect-free high-quality AlN/GaN interface is formed in H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP, thereby further significantly reducing these DBs and demonstrating the best interface performance. Experimental and calculated results confirm that all these interface defects are acceptors, which trap electrons and cause a positive threshold voltage shift. Therefore, H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP achieves the lowest density of interface acceptor defects, culminating in fabricated devices with superior threshold voltage stability compared to those with N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP.

title: High-Performance Flexible ITZO-Based Deep-Ultraviolet Phototransistors for Retina-Inspired Vision Sensors
authors: Delang Lin, Guijun Li, Man Chun Tseng, Fion Sze Yan Yeung, Hoi Sing Kwok, Rongsheng Chen
abstract: Retina-inspired vision sensors can extract input visual information through preprocessing operations to reduce system power consumption and latency. Metal–oxide thin-film transistor (MOTFT)-based phototransistors are attractive for in-sensor computing, but are often limited by sluggish temporal response, constraining high-frequency and time-dependent operation. To address this constraint, a flexible InSnMgO (ITMO)/InSnZnO (ITZO)/ITMO heterojunction deep-ultraviolet phototransistor (HDPT) was developed as an active vision sensor that exhibited stable photosynaptic behavior and emulated retinal light adaptation. Under 5-ms deep-ultraviolet (DUV) pulses, the device sustained 100-Hz operation with the 26-fJ/spike single-event energy and enabled 4-bit temporal sequence recognition. By combining blockwise superpixel aggregation and the HDPT-enabled 4-bit temporal encoding, the network input dimension was reduced to 25% of the original size while achieving over 95% accuracy in static image recognition. For dynamic perception, a 95% accuracy was achieved on dynamic gesture recognition. These results indicate hardware-level feature encoding and dimensionality reduction at the point of capture, enabling low-power, dynamic in-sensor vision.

title:
Highly Sensitive Ag Nanoparticle Deposited TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Thin Film-Based Broadband Photodetector
authors: Laishram Premjit Singh, Romesh Laishram, Sapam Bikesh, Bimoljit Chanam, Trupti Ranjan Lenka, Tanjim Rahman, Hieu P. Nguyen, Biraj Shougaijam
abstract: In this work, Ag-nanoparticles were deposited on TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-thin film (Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF) over the p-type Si (100) substrate through the simple glancing angle deposition (GLAD) method for advanced photodetector applications. The FE-SEM analysis manifests that the Ag nanoparticles (NPs) were successfully fabricated over the surface of the TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> thin films (TFs). Furthermore, the XRD analysis confirmed the presence of a pure polycrystalline nature of anatase TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> and Ag. The Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> TF sample exhibits enhanced photon absorption as compared to the bare TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF sample in the ultraviolet (UV) and visible region. Under the illumination of 390 nm, the Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF-based photodetector device gives excellent performance metrics such as responsivity (R), external quantum efficiency (EQE), detectivity (D), and noise equivalent power (NEP) of ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ ~74.25$</tex-math> </inline-formula> A/W, ~236.08%, ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 2.74\times 10^{{13}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> Jones, and ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 5.84\times 10^{-{12}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> W, respectively, at +3 V. Furthermore, the photosensitivity of ~3.36 and rise/fall times of ~98/95 ms are obtained from Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF-based device. Moreover, the Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF-based device gives good responsivity values of ~27.48 and 11.53 A/W under 532 nm (green) and 685 nm (red), respectively. These results confirm that photodetectors based on Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF fabricated using the GLAD technique can be suitable for broadband photodetector applications.

title: Dynamically Continuous Tunable Metasurface With Switchable Transmission and Absorption Modes
authors: Zhe Qin, Kaihao Zhou, Yongfeng Li, Jiechu Liu, Lixin Jiang, Wanwan Yang, Hao Yang, Zhihao Guo, Lin Zheng, Hongya Chen, Wenjie Wang, Jiafu Wang, Hua Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: The control of electromagnetic (EM) waves, especially over transmission, reflection, and absorption, is a cornerstone for advanced EM systems such as smart radomes and adaptive camouflage. However, achieving continuous control over these three responses within the same frequency band remains a significant challenge for active metasurfaces. Existing designs often suffer from discrete switching, narrow bandwidth, high insertion loss, and operating in separate bands. Therefore, this article proposes a novel active metasurface capable of dynamically and continuously regulating transmission and absorption responses in the same operational band. The metasurface comprises two key modules: an active frequency-selective layer and an active absorption layer. By biasing p-i-n diodes in their nonlinear region, the equivalent impedance of the absorption layer can be adjusted, enabling precise control over both the transmission loss and the ohmic loss. In the transmission-continuous-modulation mode, the EM response transitions from near-unity transmission to high reflection; in the absorption-continuous-modulation mode, transmission is suppressed, and reflection is tuned from perfect reflection to near-total absorption. Both simulations and measurements validate the metasurface’s performance, showing a wide −1dB transmission band and a broad −10 dB absorption band, with continuous tunability under bias voltage control. The proposed strategy offers a unified, low-profile, and versatile solution for developing next-generation adaptive EM systems.

title: Why Shorter Time Steps Induce the Failure Probability Curse in Power Outage Risk Analysis: An Explanation and Solution
authors: Jiawei Wang, Qinglai Guo, Hongbin Sun
abstract: Recent climate change has led to more frequent and widespread extreme weather events, causing damage to power system components and resulting in significant outages and losses. Component fragility curves and sequential Monte Carlo simulations have been widely used for power system outage risk assessment. Fragility curves serve as the bridge linking weather events to component failures, while sequential Monte Carlo methods capture the temporal dynamics of weather and balance probabilities with consequences. However, a direct and improper combination of these two tools can introduce significant bias, especially under high time resolution. This phenomenon is referred to as the “failure probability curse.” This paper analyzes the fundamental cause of this issue and proposes a conditional-probability correction method for the sequential Monte Carlo simulation. The method can eliminate the inherent bias from high-time-resolution sampling in power outage risk analysis and reconcile sequential Monte Carlo with different physical failure mechanisms. Furthermore, the conditional-probability perspective provided in this paper offers support and explanation for related existing methods at the level of mathematical principles, enhancing the insights of the “failure probability curse” from both an explanation and solution perspective. Numerical experiments based on an illustrative system and the Tennessee power network confirm the validity of the proposed approach.

title: An Unsupervised Image Dehazing with Scene Geometry Prior for Road Traffic Scenarios
authors: Mingye Ju, Tianyi Lyu, Chunming He, Qingshan Liu, Kai-Kuang Ma
abstract: Despite advances in single image dehazing, robust dehazing for real-world road traffic scenes remains challenging due to scarce paired data, traffic-specific geometry, and real-time constraints. To address this issue, we propose a novel image prior for road traffic scenes, termed scene geometry prior (SGP), which leverages depth cues derived from vanishing point (VP) to provide geometry-aware guidance and reduce reliance on paired training data. Our SGP comprises two components: a global SGP (G-SGP) that captures the global geometric distribution and a non-local SGP (NL-SGP) that corrects the errors, among obstructions belonging to the same category, in captured global distribution. Building on the proposed prior, we develop a lightweight and unsupervised road traffic image dehazing network (RTDnet). It consists of a main sub-network guided by the G-SGP to reconstruct the haze-free image, alongside two auxiliary sub-networks that leverage the NL-SGP and VP information to respectively estimate transmission map, and atmospheric light. During training, we introduce an atmospheric scattering model (ASM)-driven mutual-boost learning mechanism (ASM-ML), which is rooted in Bayesian theory and effectively integrates the strengths of different priors without mutual interference while distilling ASM-based physical knowledge into each subnetwork. By coupling SGP with ASM-ML, RTDnet can be trained without paired traffic data by exploiting traffic-specific geometry, whose accurate guidance reduces the reliance on large model capacity and enables lightweight real-time deployment. Experiments demonstrate that our RTDnet surpasses state-of-the-art competitors in terms of restoration quality, efficiency, and model size. Moreover, its robust dehazing performance benefits downstream tasks operating in hazy conditions.

title: Background-Oriented Schlieren Velocimetry of Helium Coolant Flow in Additively Manufactured Channels
authors: Cody S. Wiggins, Yuqiao Fan, Chris Crawford
abstract: High-pressure helium gas cooling is an attractive solution for thermal management of the fusion blanket first wall, as this coolant is chemically and neutronically inert and separable from hydrogenic species. However, due to the low thermal mass of helium, geometric optimization of these channels is required to provide sufficient cooling at manageable flow rates and pumping burdens. Increasingly, analysis and optimization of these coolant channels rely on computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations, and these require relevant experimental data for turbulence model validation. Toward this end, a high-pressure helium gas flow visualization system has been employed to image the flow of helium in flow channels with one-sided heating, mimicking the blanket first wall environment. Flow of helium at 4 MPa pressure and flow rates up to 68 g/s (Reynolds number 57 000) is supplied to rectangular channel test sections, with uniform heating applied to the bottom wall of the channel at heat fluxes varied between roughly 50 and 130 kW/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. A high-speed camera is used to image index of refraction (IOR) gradients in the fluid via background-oriented schlieren (BOS), and temperature and pressure instrumentation are used to characterize thermal-hydraulic performance of each channel. Cross correlation of time-resolved BOS images is then used to calculate time-averaged 2-D helium velocity fields. Flow in additively manufactured (AM) channels is examined in this manner, including both featureless channels and those containing baffling as a heat transfer enhancement. The flow distribution seen in the featureless case differs significantly from that seen in prior simulations, whereas the flow in the baffled case shows the predicted behavior of flow forced along the heated wall. This augmented flow distribution is seen to increase the heat transfer coefficient in the baffled test section. Strategies are discussed for ongoing and future validation of these simulations, with the aim of model deployment for blanket cooling design and optimization.

title: BC-AEAD: Blockchain-enabled Lightweight AEAD Combiner for IoT Device Authentication
authors: Aswani Devi Aguru, Suresh Babu Erukala
abstract: The enormous proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) networks necessitates robust and ultra-lightweight authentication mechanisms. Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) schemes are pivotal for resource-constrained devices, but centralized models suffer from a single point of failure. We propose BC-AEAD, a Blockchain-enabled IoT authentication and Authenticated Key Establishment (AKE) scheme using a novel AEAD combiner. BC-AEAD strategically amalgamates the high-performance tweakable block cipher primitive of LOCUS-AEAD with the resource-efficient sponge construction of ASCON. Leveraging Blockchain enables decentralized and tamper-proof key management of the AEAD combiner. The robustness of proposed combiner is validated using an informal security analysis, the Real-and-Random Oracle Model (ROR), and the Scyther tool. The performance of Blockchain network is evaluated regarding throughput, latency, and resource consumption using Hyperledger Caliper v0.4.2. The comparison of computation and communication costs of AKE schemes with state-of-the-art works is accomplished using the Pycrypto toolkit. The cost analysis of BC-AEAD on FPGA proves its practical deployability on highly constrained IoT devices with 1146 LUTs, 428 slices, and 1.86 Gbps. Eventually, the lack of a direct precedent strongly evaluates the core novelty of our unique AEAD combiner.

title: HKT-SmartAudit: Distilling Lightweight Models for Smart Contract Auditing
authors: Zhiyuan Wei, Jing Sun, Zijian Zhang, Zhe Hou, Xianhao Zhang, Meng Li, Yuqiang Sun, Daoyuan Wu, Yang Liu, Chunmiao Li, Mingchao Wan, Jin Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The rapid growth of blockchain technology has driven the widespread adoption of smart contracts; however, their inherent vulnerabilities have led to significant financial losses. Traditional auditing methods, while essential, struggle to keep pace with the increasing complexity and scale of smart contracts. Large language models (LLMs) offer promising capabilities for automating vulnerability detection, but their adoption is often limited by high computational costs. Although prior work has explored leveraging large models through agents or workflows, relatively little attention has been given to improving the performance of smaller, fine-tuned models—a critical factor for achieving both efficiency and data privacy. In this paper, we introduce HKT-SmartAudit, a framework for developing lightweight models optimized for smart contract auditing. It features a multi-stage knowledge distillation pipeline that integrates classical distillation, external domain knowledge, and reward-guided learning to transfer high-quality insights from large teacher models. A single-task learning strategy is employed to train compact student models that maintain high accuracy and robustness while significantly reducing computational overhead. Experimental results show that our distilled models outperform both commercial tools and larger models in detecting complex vulnerabilities and logical flaws, offering a practical, secure, and scalable solution for smart contract auditing. The source code is available in the GitHub repository<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: Design of a Passive Load-Bearing Exoskeleton With Reduced Joint Misalignment and Leg Interaction Forces_supp4-3675446.mp4
authors: xinyu wu
abstract: Load-bearing exoskeletons offer assistance in military, firefighting, and logistics fields. In such scenarios, weights are carried across uneven terrains. Therefore, an exoskeleton that efficiently transfers loads to the ground, adapts to versatile gaits, and minimally impacts leg movement is desired. This article presents a passive load-bearing exoskeleton to achieve these objectives. The passive exoskeleton operates without external power, transfers the load to the ground and redirects interaction forces from the thigh to the waist, and reduces potential impact on leg motion. A hip force-restoring mechanism and an optimized knee four-bar linkage were developed to minimize misalignment, enhancing human–machine kinematic compatibility. Experiments on five participants showed that the exoskeleton can reduce misalignment by 42.55% during load-bearing walking and 79.68% when squatting. The thigh interaction force was decreased by 55.65%, and load-transfer efficiency reached 60.10%. Compared to commercial load-bearing exoskeletons, there was a significant reduction in metabolic cost both indoors and outdoors, showing the exoskeleton's effectiveness in various terrain conditions.

title: Fast Human-Robot Handover using a Vision-Based Pipeline_supp1-3680049.pdf
authors: Enrico Turco

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning based Deferred Entanglement Path Selection in Quantum Networks
authors: Xiaoyu Wang, Yangming Zhao, Enshu Wang, Chen Tian, Kun Yang, Chunming Qiao
abstract: Conventional entanglement routing approaches decide the Entanglement Paths (EPs) to establish Entanglement Connections (ECs) before trying to create Entanglement Links (ELs). By doing so, very few EL failures will result in a low network throughput. In this paper, we study how to choose the EPs to establish ECs after knowing which ELs are successfully created. This is called the Deferred EP Selection (DEPS) problem. DEPS is a generalized integer multi-commodity flow problem and we cannot solve it quickly with conventional optimization methods. To address this issue, we propose a Deep Reinforcement Learning based EP Selection (DRLEPS) approach. The salient features of DRLEPS include (i) by controlling the number of candidate EPs, DRLEPS can achieve a trade-off between time complexity and the EC establishment rate; and (ii) using candidate EPs as input, DRLEPS is robust to request variation; and (iii) by training neural networks with different topologies, a model derived by DRLEPS can be applied to various networks (even with a different number of nodes) without fine-tune. Through extensive simulations, we show that even in a network with 200 nodes, DRLEPS can solve the DEPS problem in 0.39 seconds with a Nvidia GeForce 3090 GPU. It outperforms the approach always establishing ECs through the EP with the largest success probability by up to 23.4% in EC establishment rate. It also outperforms the Integer Linear Programming (ILP) based scheme, which can achieve the maximum EC establishment rate, by up to 184.2x in network throughput.

title: A Robust Active Noise Control System Based on Logarithmic Hyperbolic Secant Adaptive Filter
authors: Gunuru Harsha Vardhan Naidu, Krishna Kumar
abstract: Conventional active noise control (ANC) methods experience performance deterioration when exposed to impulsive noise (IN). Despite its transient nature, IN poses significant risks to auditory health. Recent approaches employing the maximum correntropy criterion (MCC) and its generalized version (GMCC) have attracted considerable interest for impulsive noise suppression. Nevertheless, these methods exhibit substantial steady-state misalignment, degrading their noise cancellation performance. To enhance ANC performance under impulsive conditions, this work introduces a novel robust filtered-x logarithmic hyperbolic secant adaptive filter (FxLHSAF) that minimizes a logarithmic hyperbolic secant loss function. Furthermore, an affine combination of FxLHSAF (A-FxLHSAF) is developed to attain accelerated convergence and reduced residual error. Experimental simulations demonstrate the superiority of both proposed techniques over other existing algorithms, with FxLHSAF and A-FxLHSAF delivering optimal noise suppression performance.

title: One Size Fits All: Achieving Generic and Communication-Efficient Federated Multi-Task Learning via Bi-level Sparsification
authors: Wentai Wu, Chiyuan Tan, Zhetao Li, Saiqin Long, Pengpeng Qiao, Zhirun Zheng, Weiwei Lin, Yang Cao
abstract: Federated Multi-Task Learning (FMTL) has demon-strated significant potential in benefiting data owners that per-form different tasks collaboratively in privacy-sensitive scenarios. However, existing FMTL methods struggle with the negative transfer that arises from the knowledge interference especially with over-parameterized models. This hinders both efficient knowledge transfer and the model's ability to capture inter-task correlations. To address these issues, we cast light on the power of sparsity, atop the highly modular Multi-gate Mixture-of-Experts (MMoE), to simultaneously realize effective separation and communication-efficient exchange of cross-domain knowl-edge over a generic architecture. With bi-level sparsification mechanism combining sparse expert activation with gradient-driven parameter masking, our approach offers a generic, extend-able, and interpretable framework by simultaneously achieving (1) structural mitigation of task interference by isolating knowl-edge pathways, and (2) significant reduction of communication overhead without architectural adaptation or explicit compres-sion. Experimental results demonstrate that, without relying on heavy models, our framework improves both model accuracy and convergence speed while cutting communication traffic by approximately 70% via the bi-level sparsification design.

title: Optimal Closed-Loop Control of Dual-Active Bridge Type DC-DC and Single-Stage DC-AC Converters With Multiple Degrees-of-Freedom
authors: Ivan Z. Petrić, Rahul Mallik, Milan Ilić
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: This paper introduces a method for optimal closed-loop control of DAB-type DC-DC and single-stage high-frequency link DC-AC converters with multiple degrees-of-freedom (DoF). The proposed single-input multiple-output (SIMO) architecture distributes the control effort among the DoF based on the gradient of the regulated output. This minimally perturbs the control input vector, thus reducing the jump discontinuities in the control variables and suppressing transient oscillations. Additionally, the SIMO control normalizes the loop gain to impose constant dynamics throughout the operating range. Finally, a saturation-handling algorithm is proposed to retain optimal distribution even if a control variable is saturated, allowing full controllability and constant small-signal dynamics as long as at least one DoF remains. A small-signal model is derived and validated in simulations. Experimental validations are presented on an 850 W series-resonant DAB prototype that is re-configurable for DC-DC and DC-AC operation.

title: Feeling the Facts: Real-time wearable fact-checkers can use nudges to reduce user belief in false information
authors: Chitralekha Gupta, Nadia Victoria Aritonang, Dixon Prem Daniel Rajendran, Valdemar Danry, Pattie Maes, Suranga Nanayakkara
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
abstract: None

title: Standing on the Giants: Informative Messenger Prompts with Self-adapter for Image Restoration_supp1-3677280.pdf
authors: Lei Zhu

title: Regionally Routed Attention U-Net With Latent Fading Kalman Filtering for Real-Time EIT_supp1-3675106.mp4
authors: ZIJUN LONG
abstract: Real-time electrical impedance tomography requires reconstruction algorithms that reconcile high structural fidelity with strict latency constraints. We propose RNU-Net, a high-performance, end-to-end time-difference EIT reconstruction framework based on a specialized 1-D ConvNeXt architecture augmented with regionally routed attention. To enable robust mapping, the model was trained on a large-scale corpus of 100 000 samples generated via EIDORS, encompassing inclusions of varying sizes and conductivities with canonical geometries (circles, squares, and right-angled triangles). To further enhance robustness in low-SNR environments, the framework integrates a latent fading Kalman filter for dynamic sensor-domain denoising. On the canonical synthetic test set, RNU-Net attains a high reconstruction accuracy (SSIM = 0.9738). Furthermore, we rigorously evaluate the model’s generalization capacity through a transfer learning strategy using a novel stochastic high-order Fourier benchmark, which introduces topological fusion to mimic complex structures. Evaluations on physical tank data and independent test set (KIT4) demonstrate that the framework maintains robust cross-domain adaptability. In terms of efficiency, the framework achieves an inference throughput of approximately 54.5 FPS on a mobile GPU and 12.4 FPS on a standard CPU, providing a practical solution for high-precision, real-time EIT monitoring.

title: Finite Time and Robust $k$-Winners-Take-All Neural Network for Multiagents Coordination
authors: Jun Cai, Jinghan Liu, Lin Xiao, Chenfu Yi
abstract: In order to better adapt to the needs of practical scenarios, multiagents task allocation systems need to be able to select winners in the shortest possible time under external interference. Therefore, this article proposes a finite time and robust <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>-winners-take-all (FTR-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA) neural network, by introducing a time-varying learning rate and a versatile activation function into the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA neural network. Compared with existing <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA neural networks, this network has faster finite-time convergence speed and noise suppression ability. The global convergence, finite-time convergence, and robustness of the proposed network have been demonstrated theoretically. Finally, these performances of the FTR-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA neural<bold xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"/> network are further validated through simulation results. In addition, the multiagents system is simulated using E-puck robots on the CoppeliaSim platform, serving to confirm the efficacy of the FTR-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA neural network.

authors: N/A

title: CSAP-YOLOv8: A Cross-Scale Adaptive Perception Network for Ship Detection in Synthetic Aperture Radar Images
authors: Jiehong Wu, Congcong Wan, Lijun Gao, Cuiwei Liu, Chengcheng Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: With the increasing frequency of maritime activities, ship detection based on Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery plays a vital role in maritime surveillance and safety. However, in high-resolution SAR sea scenes, vessel targets typically exhibit characteristics such as small scale, slender structures, and dense distribution. Simultaneously, the influence of complex sea clutter and multi-scale variations poses significant challenges for existing detection methods in terms of feature representation consistency, cross-scale fusion, and precise localization.To address this, this paper proposes a Cross-Scale Adaptive Path YOLOv8 (CSAP-YOLOv8) vessel detection framework aimed at enhancing the robustness and generalization capabilities of SAR vessel detection in complex marine environments. Starting from the feature representation source, this method enhances the sustained characterization capability for small-scale and slender targets through cross-scale receptive field modeling. Simultaneously, it introduces an adaptive feature fusion mechanism to achieve semantic alignment and complementarity between features at different levels, mitigating semantic offset issues in multi-scale feature fusion. Furthermore, to address the instability of bounding box regression caused by SAR vessel geometry, a task-aware regression loss function is designed to improve localization accuracy through spatial position and shape constraints.Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a 3.5% improvement in AP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50–95</sub> over baseline models on the HRSID dataset and yields even more significant performance gains on the RSDD-SAR dataset, validating its effectiveness and strong generalization capabilities across varying resolutions and complex scene conditions.

title: Hybrid TD3-PI Control for Wireless Charging of Light Electric Vehicles
authors: Akanksha, Afraz Ahmad, Ilamparithi Thirumarai Chelvan, Khanh Dat Truong
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-assisted adaptive control strategy for wireless power transfer (WPT) in light electric vehicle (LEV) charging. A phase-shifted full-bridge converter with series–series compensation is considered, where the primary-side controller regulates the secondary charging current. Conventional proportional–integral (PI) control, tuned around nominal coupling, suffers performance degradation and even loss of convergence under coil misalignment and coupling variation. To address this, a Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) agent is used to adapt the PI gains online based on secondary-side feedback.The agent is trained in a Simulink environment and deployed in inference-only mode on a TI C2000 F28379D microcontroller, occupying approximately 25% of on-chip memory, demonstrating the feasibility of DRL-based adaptive control on resource-constrained embedded platforms. Experimental results show that the proposed TD3-assisted adaptive PI achieves constant-current regulation with significantly faster convergence compared to fixed-gain PI, settling time is reduced from 8.65 ms to 5.95 ms under aligned conditions, from 17.00 ms to 11.05 ms under lateral misalignment, and from 26.00 ms to 14.82 ms under angular misalignment, corresponding to improvements of about 35% and 43%. The faster transient response lowers stress and losses in the power switches. These results validate that DRL-enabled adaptive control enhances robustness of WPT-based LEV charging under realistic misalignment scenarios while remaining implementable on industrial-grade digital controllers.

title: The Contribution of Service-Learning to the Holistic Development of Engineering Students in Private Higher Education_supp1-3682724.pdf
authors: Thiago J. Luz

title: Optimization of Diverging-Wave Imaging in Ultrasound: Virtual Source Configuration and Synthetic Aperture Strategies
authors: Jaebum Park, Doyoung Jang, Heechul Yoon, Tai-Kyong Song
abstract: Diverging-wave imaging (DWI) is a promising ultrafast ultrasound technique that enables wide-field acquisition at very high frame rates. In this study, we present a unified analytical framework for DWI, focusing on two synthetic aperture reception strategies: diverging-wave synthetic aperture focusing (DW-SAF) and diverging-wave synthetic focusing (DW-SF). Using a generalized beam-field model, we investigate how virtual source configuration—defined by aperture size, source spacing, and source location—influence spatial resolution, grating lobe (GL) artifacts, noise characteristics, and temporal resolution. Theoretical predictions are validated through Field-II simulations, tissue-mimicking phantom experiments, and in vivo studies. Results demonstrate that DW-SAF achieves higher spatial resolution but generates more prominent GL artifacts, while DW-SF mitigates GL visibility at the expense of reduced resolution. Quantitative assessments highlight trade-offs in resolution, contrast, and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) as functions of the number and arrangement of virtual sources. Based on these findings, we provide practical guidelines for selecting virtual source parameters and reception strategies to optimize DWI sequences tailored to diverse clinical requirements.

title: PHMRNet: Persistent Homology based Mamba-RWKV Network for LiDAR Place Recognition
authors: Dejing Zhou, Xinyu Jiang, Sitao Chen, Zhonghao Cai, Jin Wu, Xieyuanli Chen, Jufeng Yang, Chi-Man Vong
abstract: LiDAR-based place recognition (LPR) is a key component of visual localization and autonomous driving. Although LiDAR data are usually preprocessed by motion undistortion, which can greatly reduce scene distortion caused by sensor motion, 3-dimensional (3D) point clouds in complex scenes still show inherent unstructured sparsity. Projecting 3D point clouds into 2D range view (RV) images can provide structured feature representations that are more suitable for neural networks and deep learning methods. However, this strategy has two main limitations: 1) the important geometric details are removed by the projection, impairing the effective extraction and representation of scene features. 2) unpredictable occlusions and viewpoint changes alter the observed scene structure and often lead to incorrect place matches. To address these limitations, we propose a LPR network that combines topological information extracted by persistent homology (PH) with RV features to compensate for missing structural information and produce more stable descriptors. Specifically, PH-stable features are added as a topology-aware sampling channel for the RV feature to integrate more stable and discriminative robust geometric cues. Next, we introduce a spatiotemporal fusion module that links topological and RV features across consecutive frames, thereby improving spatiotemporal consistency of the representation and thus reducing the impact of occlusions and rotations. Compared with OverlapMamba, PHMRNet can better handle spatial, temporal, and geometric features, improving the robustness of the descriptor. Experiments on public datasets show that PHMRNet outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy and robustness under the same driving conditions and LiDAR scanning rate.

title: Privacy Preserving Alzheimer’s Disease Classification using Multi-head Attention Transfer via Adaptive Temperature Distillation with Federated Learning
authors: S Sabari Vasan, P Jayalakshmi
abstract: The implementation of artificial intelligence in medical imaging, challenges on se- curity of the data and institutional collaboration is major. This research proposes a new research framework that uses a combination of the Multi-Head Attention Transfer (MHAT) and the Adaptive Temperature Distillation (ATD) to carry out privacypreserving classification of Alzheimer’s disease using federated learning, as is the first time in the world. Our model utilizes the nature of heterogeneity in distributed medical imaging data by using temperature-scaled knowledge distillation between heterogeneous client models and a centralized server architecture. The framework does implement a dual-phase training methodology: clients are trained on private datasets with theirwarm-up training and then had iteratively exchanging knowledge through soft label aggregation. The dynamic variation of this distillation intensity (from 5.0 as an initial value with exponential decay) as an adaptive temperature mechanism is implemented to optimize knowledge transfer through 30 rounds of federated rounds. Experimental validation on Multi-class Alzheimer’s disease dataset shows an exceptional performance which shows 82.25% Accurcy, 82.44% Precision, 82.32% Recall, 82.32% F1-score and a remarkable score of 95.30% for ROC-AUC. The framework has an outstanding communication efficiency with an average of 176MB per round and a computational efficiency of 2.2 seconds per client on average. Fairness analysis shows very small variability (0.09) among the client’s performances, which also means a fair distribution of knowledge among heterogeneous architectures. This work composes a new development in the field of privacy-preserving medical AI, showing that with federated knowledge distillation, centralized modeling performance can be achieved without sharing data and staying strictly for the data location.

title: Evaluation of Support Vector Machine and Random Forest Models for EMG-Based Knee Motion Phase Classification
authors: Ing Teck Phang, Asnor Juraiza Ishak, Siti Anom Ahmad, Tomohiro Shibata, Slamet Riyadi
abstract: Knee-related conditions, such as anterior cruciate ligament injuries and meniscal ruptures, significantly affect mobility and quality of life. Although range-of-motion exercises are important for rehabilitation, interpreting electromyography signals related to these knee motions remains challenging owing to the complexity and variability of muscle activation patterns. This study investigated the classification of knee motion phases (resting, holding, and flexion/extension) by using surface electromyography signals from the biceps femoris and semitendinosus muscles in subjects with and without knee issues. Electromyography signals were preprocessed by removing outliers using the median absolute deviation and Kalman filtering. The motion phases were classified using a Support Vector Machine and Random Forest models within a subject-independent leave-one-subject-out evaluation framework. For subjects without knee issues, Random Forest performed with higher classification accuracy than Support Vector Machine for both muscles, with significant differences confirmed by McNemar’s test (p < 0.05). For subjects with knee issues, both classifiers performed with comparable mean accuracies of approximately 90% for both muscles, with no statistically significant difference between models (p > 0.05), despite the Random Forest exhibiting slightly more temporally coherent time-series segmentation. The results demonstrate that electromyography-based motion phase classification is feasible under strict subject-independent evaluation. Random Forest showed greater robustness in healthy subjects, whereas both classifiers performed equivalently under pathological conditions, enhancing their potential application in intelligent rehabilitation and clinical motion assessment systems.

title: Reading Between the Lines: A Comprehensive Survey of Lane Detection Datasets for Autonomous Driving
authors: Felix Grün
abstract: Lane detection is a fundamental component of perception systems in autonomous driving and advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS). While deep learning has significantly improved the performance of lane detection algorithms, these advancements rely heavily on access to high-quality annotated data. However, existing lane detection datasets vary widely in scope, structure, and annotation quality, creating challenges for researchers interested in gaining an overview of the field. In this article, we present a structured in-depth survey of publicly available lane detection datasets, spanning early benchmark collections to recent large-scale, multimodal datasets. We use a scenario-based evaluation framework to assess the practical utility of dataset annotations in supporting driving tasks such as lane following, lane changes, and intersection navigation. Furthermore, we propose a structured approach for evaluating dataset diversity and bias. By combining a systematic qualitative review of sampled images with a quantitative analysis, we provide a comprehensive assessment of dataset variability and coverage. Our findings reveal significant disparities in dataset content, bias, and suitability for complex driving scenarios. We identify critical limitations in annotation schemes, diversity coverage, and the underrepresentation of challenging conditions. This work serves as both a practical reference for researchers and a call to action for the development of more balanced, diverse, and robust datasets that better meet the safety and generalizability demands of real-world autonomous driving systems.

title: Machine Learning Enhanced Hybrid Microwave Biosensor for High-Sensitivity Glucose Detection in Fruit Juices
authors: Idowu A. Ojerinde, Adel B. Abdel-Rahman, Haruichi Kanaya, Ahmed Allam
abstract: Rapid assessment of glucose in physiologically and commercially consumed liquids is important to health and food-quality monitoring. However, conventional enzyme-based methods are invasive and inefficient for continuous or real-time glucose quantification. This paper proposes a miniature U-shaped microwave biosensor integrated with an interdigital capacitor (IDC) and fabricated on a Rogers RO4003C substrate for in vitro dielectric characterization of glucose-based liquids. To realize bandpass response and enhance selectivity, a 1.5 pF series capacitor is incorporated into the feedline. The sensor demonstrates distinct and sharp resonance dips in both reflection coefficient (S11) and transmission coefficient (S21), validating its strong resonant response suitable for dielectric sensing. Glucose-water solutions with concentrations of 5%, 10%, 15%, and 25% were measured, demonstrating minimal resonant frequency drift and a repeatable 0.8 dB variation in the magnitude of S11, corresponding to an average sensitivity of approximately 0.05 dB/% glucose. Comparative measurements of fresh and processed mango, guava, orange, and grape fruit juices indicated that different S-parameter responses are related to the concentration of sugar and the effect of processing. For automated data interpretation, a hybrid machine learning (ML) framework combining Random Forest (RF)–based feature selection, XGBoost refinement, and artificial neural network (ANN) classification was employed for juice-type discrimination. Glucose concentration is predicted using a deep neural network (128–64–32–1), achieving MSE of 0.2297, MAE of 0.21%, and R2 of 0.979, with over 95% of predictions within ±2% error, demonstrating reliable in vitro glucose sensing and food-quality assessment.

title: Lumped-Parameter Transmission-Line Modeling Via Kron Reduction
authors: Hyeonjung Jung, Andrew McEniry, Sairaj Dhople
abstract: For transmission lines modeled as the series combination of an infinite number of (lossy) <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$RLC$</tex-math></inline-formula> circuits (each modeling an infinitesimally short segment), the ubiquitous <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Pi$</tex-math></inline-formula>-equivalent-circuit lumped-parameter model in sinusoidal steady state is typically derived by manipulating Telegrapher's equations. We present an alternate analytical approach that leverages Kron reduction to eliminate all but terminal nodes from the originating network of series-connected two-port segments.

title: Vision-Aided Multi-Stream Hybrid Beamforming for Millimeter Wave MIMO Systems
authors: Tianqi Xiang, Ji Gu, Yicheng Wang, Yuehong Gao, Xin Zhang, Chongjun Ouyang
abstract: Vision-aided beamforming helps reduce the overhead of millimeter-wave multi-antenna systems, but its extension to multi-stream multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) transmission remains challenging and its adaptation to unknown user terminal orientation is limited. In this work, a vision-aided multi-stream hybrid beamforming framework is proposed, which comprises a multi-branch neural network that predicts transmit beams from visual inputs and a sequential sparse recovery approach that estimates the receive combiners. Numerical results based on the ViWi dataset demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves significant multi-stream capacity gains over vision-based single-stream beam prediction methods, while also reducing beam training overhead compared with wireless-only baselines.

title: MAFNet-DETR: Multi-branch Adaptive Fusion Network for Small Object Detection in UAV Aerial Images
authors: Fang Guo, Hengyi Xin, Zhili Chen, Luyi Hou, Pengqiao Chen, Lin Wang, Junyi Ye, Dewang Chen
abstract: Small object detection in high-altitude unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery presents formidable challenges, including severely constrained target scale and intricate background interference. To address these issues, this paper proposes MAFNet-DETR, a novel detection framework built upon RT-DETR-R18, designed to augment feature representation capability on densely populated small-object benchmarks such as VisDrone2019. MAFNet-DETR systematically advances detection performance through four core architectural innovations: the Three-branch Gated Enhancement Block (TGEB), which employs large-kernel convolutions to capture expansive global contextual information in conjunction with a gating mechanism for adaptive salient feature modulation, substantially elevating the model’s perceptual sensitivity to sparse and inconspicuous signals; the Global-Local Spatial Attention (GLSA) module, which adopts a dual-branch parallel architecture to independently extract fine-grained local spatial details and long-range global dependencies, thereby achieving an effective equilibrium between local perceptual acuity and holistic contextual comprehension; the Adaptive Bidirectional Feature Pyramid Network (Adaptive-BiFPN), which incorporates a dual-weight fusion mechanism coupling spatially adaptive weights with globally learnable weights to realize fine-grained and computationally efficient multi-scale feature aggregation, significantly mitigating the degradation of small-object detail information during hierarchical feature propagation; and the Lightweight Progressive Upsampling Block (LPUB), which achieves high-fidelity feature map resolution restoration through the synergistic integration of depthwise separable convolutions, channel shuffling, and shift channel mixing operations. Extensive experimental evaluations conducted on three publicly available benchmark datasets—VisDrone2019, SIMD, and AI-TOD—demonstrate that, relative to the baseline RT-DETR-R18, the proposed method achieves improvements in mAP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</sub> of 6.1%, 4.6%, and 4.8%, and in mAP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50−95</sub> of 4.5%, 2.7%, and 3.3%, respectively, while concurrently reducing the parameter count by 12.46%, 8.7%, and 9.45%. The proposed model attains substantial gains in detection accuracy and exhibits strong applicability to small object detection under complex background conditions.

title: Depth from Focus via Test-time Optimization of Monocular Depth Estimation Models
authors: Jun Kohashiguchi, Yuki Fujimura, Kazuya Kitano, Takuya Funatomi, Yasuhiro Mukaigawa
abstract: Depth from focus (DFF) estimates scene depth by analyzing images captured at different focus distances. Recent deep learning–based DFF methods can predict depth at a metric scale; however, their accuracy is often limited by the relatively small amount of available training data. To overcome this limitation, we propose a more accurate DFF framework that leverages prior knowledge from monocular depth estimation (MDE) models trained on large-scale datasets. Specifically, at test time, the output of a existing DFF method is used as a reference, and the parameters of the MDE model are optimized on a per-scene basis. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method improves both depth accuracy and structural quality, and achieves consistent improvement across a wide range of scenes compared to existing DFF approaches.

title: Angle-Insensitive Dual-Layer Metasurface Color Filters for Imaging Device
authors: Ki Won Jeong, Yun Seon Do
abstract: High-resolution color filtering is essential for advanced imaging devices, yet conventional dye-based filters exhibit poor thermal stability and fabrication challenges at sub-micron pixel scales. Metasurface-based color filters (MCFs) offer a viable alternative through structural color formation, but suffer from spectral shifts and color distortions under oblique incidence. We present an aluminum (Al) based dual-layer metasurface color filter (DLMCF) that integrates a disk array metasurface color filter (DMCF) beneath a conventional hole array metasurface color filter (HMCF). This architecture utilizes the interaction between localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and Fabry-Pérot resonance (FPR) to achieve angle-independent color filtering. The optimized DLMCF maintains stable transmission up to 40° incidence, with an 84.3% reduction compared to 80.6 nm for conventional HMCF. The design achieves 32–36% pitch reduction relative to HMCF, enabling 117–144% improvement in pixel density.

title: Unsupervised Author Name Disambiguation Method Based on Hybrid Embedding
authors: Sangho Song, Jeonghun Seo, Christopher Retiti Diop Emane, Delin Meng, Dojin Chol, Hyeonbyeong Lee, Jongtae Lim, Kyoungsoo Bok, Jaesoo Yoo
abstract: Author Name Disambiguation (AND) is a critical task for accurately distinguishing different researchers who share identical or similar names. It directly affects the accuracy of research performance analysis. and researcher profiling. However, incomplete metadata, name variations, and the prevalence of common names limit the effectiveness of rule-based methods. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a hybrid unsupervised approach that integrates semantic and relational embeddings. We first employ SPECTER to generate semantic embeddings from titles, abstracts, and affiliations, and then construct relational embeddings by performing meta-path random walks on a heterogeneous graph built from co-author, affiliation, and venue relations, followed by Word2Vec training. The similarity matrices from both embeddings are combined and fed into HDBSCAN, which automatically determines cluster numbers and groups papers belonging to the same author. Extensive experiments on diverse real-world academic data sets show that the proposed method outperforms SND-ALL and BOND on major benchmarks, and notably achieves up to a 73% performance improvement in pairwise F1-score compared to the existing methods on the PubMed dataset.

title: Performance Evaluation of Partial Shading Detection for Photovoltaic Generation Systems: A Comparative Study of Array Configurations
authors: Jen-Hao Teng, Han-Cheng Wu, Chun-Wei Cheng
abstract: Partial Shading Detection (PSD) can determine whether PS occurs and assist in selecting the appropriate Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) algorithm, thereby improving the performance of Photovoltaic Generation Systems (PVGS). Most PSD methods focused on examining the series configuration of a PVGS. Nonetheless, the typical array configurations for a PVGS encompass Series-Parallel (SP), Total-Cross-Tied (TCT), and SP-TCT arrangements. Therefore, the effectiveness of a PSD under different PVGS array configurations needs to be investigated, as these configurations can alter a PVGS’s output curves and degrade the performance of conventional PSD methods. This paper proposes an enhanced PSD method based on the parameter estimation of a PVGS and the determined PS factor. This paper uses PVGSs composed of 3×3 and 5×5 PV panels to verify the enhanced PSD method. The performance of the enhanced PSD is then validated through various PVGS array configurations and PS patterns under different solar irradiances. PS patterns, including column, row, diagonal, and corner, are examined for the PVGS. Experimental results demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of the enhanced PSD. Since PVGS array configurations alter the output characteristic curves, tracking the Global Maximum Power Point (GMPP) is possible even when PSD results are incorrect. Simulation results indicate that the proposed enhanced PSD achieves high PSD correctness and maintains excellent GMPP correctness even when incorrect PSD results are encountered.

title: Who You Explain To Matters: Learning by Explaining to Conversational Agents with Different Pedagogical Roles
authors: Zhengtao Xu, Junti Zhang, Anthony Tang, Yi-Chieh Lee
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
